First off, thanks for all the incredible work you've done on your channel – it's been a huge help to us. I have a different issue I'd like to ask about.

author: @pgramsey1
text:
An always amazing channel. His creativity is just mind bending.

I've never messed with TinkerCad. I'm a Fusion user, after giving up on FreeCAD. Looks interesting. I'm probably going to stick with what I know but I love that snap and socket object. 

3D printed spheres are rubbish, but cylinders are great. What a great insight. 

This bearing is going to need to be lubricated or there's going to be serious wear. The path on the race contacting the top of the cylinder is longer than the bottom, but the two points on the cylinder are going to move at the same rate, so there's going to be a lot of relative motion. Grease will let it slide without galling the opposite face. White lithium or even beeswax might be enough. Depends on how long you want it to last.

author: @marsrover6121
text:
This is a great video. It's interesting to see a pretty complex design to come out of a ridamentary modeling software like TinkerCad. As an engineering student, I'm always interested in how I can leverage the unique ways that additive manufacturing systems can be used to make mechanical components. 

I was wondering, as many are, if the design would be helped by conical rollers as it seems that there might be a slight angle on every roller causing it to only contact a point surface instead of a line of contact. It seems that the main benefit of roller bearings in current practice is to use the line contact to spread the force over more area and crossed roller bearings are used commonly in precision applications where the wear is a big factor aswell as their ability to resist all three types of loading. Being made of PLA/ABS/PETG plastics the wear might be more significant even in low precision applications just because the material is much softer.

I like the snaps as a way of joining the two sides together that was a pretty clever way to do that even if it introduces extra friction. Alternatively I think it would be possible to create a housing for 3d printing that uses inner and outer rings instead of the sandwiched top and bottom. You could then have the outer ring split into two pieces for assembly and then you would have them interlock and stay together without introducing another source of friction taking down the efficiency of the bearing. I think I will investigate some possible designs on my own.

I was also wondering if anyone could explain the specific benefits of the crossed roller design. Would an inline roller design be less efficient in this case because the rollers can touch each other, minimizing the interference surface, or is this what makes the bearing better in moment or axial loading?

author: @marcus3d
text:
1:33 The problem isn't at all that 3D-printers are "digital". At all. Not even close. There's no problem adding ever smaller micro stepping and/or gearing down. After all, the printer itself is very much analog. It's a physical thing with continuous (non-discrete) movements.

The problem with near-horizontal surfaces appears if you're doing planar printing, layer on top of layer, since layers can't be infinitely thin. The limiting factor isn't the digital nature of the controller, or even the stepper motors. It's the physically practical requirements of the minimum layer height.

Now, you don't have to do planar printing, but most 3D printers are at least somewhat limited when it comes to non-planar printing, as they can't tilt the nozzle, nor the build plate.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: well we all have our view i guess

replies.author: @marcus3d
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering  Huh? Well, yes, but your view is completely wrong, and I explained why and how, and even what the symptoms are caused by. So yes, we can have our own view, and yours is definitely wrong. No ifs or buts about it.

replies.author: @SnakebitSTI
replies.text:
Part of the issue is the practical limits on minimum layer height, part of the issue is inaccuracies created by the process of starting and stopping printing on a layer, and part of the issue is bed adhesion (a sphere rests on a point geometrically and a little tiny circle physically).

Aliasing from digital to analog conversion does technically place a limit on how thin layers can be, but it isn't the limiting factor in practice. If 1 step of the z axis motor(s) is too big, you could swap to a motor with smaller steps or gear down the motor. And of course there's micro stepping, but that's complicated.

I mean, really, 3D printers are terribly imprecise machines compared to some of the stuff out there in the metrology world that is ultimately controlled by digital computers. Digital isn't the problem.

replies.author: @evancoberley2650
replies.text: ​@marcus3d 99% of consumer FDM and resin printers use discrete, planar code. The conversation is about planar printing. That much is obvious. Your argument is largely semantic, irrelevant following a simple clarification, and a little hostile?

replies.author: @marcus3d
replies.text:
@evancoberley2650  No. If the digital nature of the controller was the problem it would be equally problematic when printing cylinders on the same printer! Obviously. But cylinders aren't a problem (even on fully planar printers), because...guess what...the problem isn't that some part of the printer is digital! The OP is just completely wrong any way you look at it. The problem has nothing to do with anything being digital. Nothing at all.

(Also, printers usually run gcode, and gcode isn't inherently planar, but yeah, approximately 100% of slicing done on the planet produces planar gcode, although that's slowly changing. The newest slicers now have a non-planar feature, namely for fuzzy top skin.)

author: @Quinten0508
text:
1:30 the stepper motors only work in angular steps, that is what creates the jagged lines. However, angular momentum smooths things out, and a cylinder is still "round" in 2 out of three dimensions. A lot of effort has been put into printing these "arcs" in the XY plane, and this is really a non-issue (print a flat disk and check if you can see stairstepping for example). Instead, it's the filament lines that create a jagged surface in the Z direction in FDM printing, sometimes together with necessary support material on the underside of the sphere. Result is the same though!
3:20 the elephant's foot is already compensated for in most slicers, e.g. in prusaslicer under print settings -> advanced tab -> slicing.
Love the video, your bearing is very clever and seems to work great :D

replies.author: @SnakebitSTI
replies.text:
You do realize you're making a theoretical objection to a bearing design that is widely used in practice, right? Cross roller bearings work. Roughly speaking, they're used for high precision/load low speed applications. The rollers roll more than they slide. Of course there's _some_ sliding; it's a roller bearing not a fluid bearing. That's what lubricant is for.

Honestly, 3D printing is just not a good way to make bearings. But if you're going to do it, cross roller bearings have a solid set of tradeoffs.

author: @hashbrown777
text:
To make a regular bearing using this (inner and outer ring, instead of side by side) cut your rotated diamand against the top/bottom race' corners (cylider hole), to get a bigger and smaller ring, instead of in line with the races inner-outer diamond corners (symmetric halves).

The outer run make sure it prints in one piece (like you had your bottom/top halves print in one piece each) but allow your inner ring to print in half and use your same snap mechanism (probably use the tight fitting option if you used the free spinning before).

Place the bearings in the outer run in the same alternating pattern, then push the inner run sandwich together to lock everything inside. Done! Now your axel spins celative to the outer bearing

author: @srh2301
text: The rollers are cylindric and the surfaces they roll on are conical. The rollers will rub because of the different diameters between top and bottom. Also, the snap connection forms a sliding bearing or sliding surfaces, which reduces the purpose of the roller bearing substantially.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: they touch at a tangent - so that doesn't happen - and the snap fitting is optional

replies.author: @jdudb
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering At any point the roller surface may touch at a tangent but the traveled distance at one end of the roller tangent point is different to the other end of the same roller and the infinite number of tangent points all touch at the same time and travel different different distances/speeds so indeed do rub.  This could be alleviated by introducing tapered rollers as in standard taper roller bearings.  This design is basically two opposed taper roller bearings integrated into one unit.

replies.author: @Kargoneth
replies.text: Yes. Conical rollers of the proper dimensions are necessary to ensure uniform wear. Compare cylindrical roller thrust bearings with tapered roller thrust bearings.

replies.author: @BooBaddyBig
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering There's less circumferential distance along the raceway closer in to the axis than further out, so the rollers need to be smaller nearer the axis to roll correctly. Google 'tapered roller bearing' to find the correct geometries for them to roll with no slip.

author: @Karmabim123
text: 3D printers generally print cylinders (circles) as a set of small straight lines so they won't actually be completely round. It is possible to increase the number of these lines thus bringing it closer to a circle but that also places an increased load on the 3D printer's processor. In practice, the resultant circles (thus cylinders) produced are okay for crude bearings but it is still a limitation.

author: @ryandavis7593
text:
I was wondering if spheroid would possibly be better than a true cylinder. It would allow for some radial misalignment. 
The generators on the EMD locomotives that I work on have spheroidal bearings on one end and are supported on the other end by the engine crank. 
This gives us play in the alignment to a small degree in both thrust and radial. 
We aim for less than ten one thousandths of an inch in radial and air gap. If they weren’t spheroidal bearings we would have to have these tolerances much closer. 
Happy new year.

replies.author: @chrstphrr
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering another thing to consider - the SVG revolution tool you showed has the upper limit of 48 steps.  

You could make 48 steps across less than a full 360 degree sweep:
At 45 degrees of sweep, and copy/rotate/merge that 45 degree revolution 8-fold to get a smoother shape to render.

Now, we don't know how fine is fine enough... that's what testing is for, though!
A few iterations like this, coupled with @johnbarry8185 's suggestion to test might help yield the point of diminishing returns.

Filming the tests and results would make a lovely video follow-up to this one!

replies.author: @Guardian_Arias
replies.text:
Designing and slicing should be done in two different pieces of software since they are two very different things. However, most slicers let you do some last-minute changes like resize, cut, and combine different things into one assembly.

The most important thing about a slicer is how well it works with your printer.

The most important thing about a 3D modeling piece of software is how well it works with you. If it's not intuitive for you and you have to constantly remind yourself of what button to press on a mouse to rotate or pan an object, then skip that software and find another. Something this simple and so integral with how you interact with your 3D models should not be a source of friction in your process.

replies.text: No, respectfully, the softer filaments will fall to physics.  Always a trade off. It will work but not as well as soft will increase friction.  If you want to bounce in a cradle physics toy you don't do too soft.  Conversely If you want it to survive higher weights and speed maybe a little give will keep it from being so strong that it is brittle.  That clear soft rubbery filament, forget what its called will squash flat. T something?
